lessons.title: Displaying Text
lessons.summary: Understand the differences between println, print, and printf

contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- How to display text on the screen using **System.out.println**()
- The difference between println and print
- String concatenation (the + [[operator]])

The first step in programming is "displaying text on the screen."

contents.title: Three Ways to Display Output
contents.body: There are three main [[method]]s for displaying text in Java.
contents.note: The "ln" in println stands for "line." It automatically adds a newline after the output.

contents.title: Difference Between println and print
contents.body:
This is a common point of confusion for beginners.

| Method | Behavior | Output Example |
|---|---|---|
| println | Adds a newline after output | Hello(newline)World |
| print | Does not add a newline | HelloWorld |

println is short for "print + line." In most cases, you will use println.
contents.note: You can also add a newline with the newline character "\n" when using print, but println is simpler.

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
| Item | Description |
|---|---|
| println | Prints output followed by a newline (most commonly used) |
| print | Prints output without a newline |
| printf | Prints formatted output (%s = string, %d = integer) |

contents.title: Escape Sequences
contents.body:
To represent special characters such as newlines and tabs within a string, use **escape sequences** that start with a backslash (\).
These are used when you want to insert a newline in the middle of a string, separate from println's automatic newline.
contents.note: \n (newline) and \t (tab) are especially common. To include a double quote inside a string [[literal]], escape it with \".

contents.title: printf Format Specifiers
contents.body:
Let's learn the commonly used format specifiers for printf.

| Specifier | Meaning | Example |
|--------|------|------|
| %s | String | printf("%s", "Java") → Java |
| %d | Integer | printf("%d", 42) → 42 |
| %f | Decimal (default 6 digits) | printf("%f", 3.14) → 3.140000 |
| %.2f | Decimal (2 digits specified) | printf("%.2f", 3.14) → 3.14 |
| %n | Newline (OS-dependent newline character) | printf("Hello%n") |
contents.note: printf does not automatically add a newline. Add %n or \n at the end if you want a newline.

quizzes.question: What is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: print("A") outputs A without a newline, println("B") outputs B followed by a newline, and print("C") outputs C. The result is AB(newline)C.

quizzes.question: Which method does NOT add a newline after output?
quizzes.explanation: print() outputs without a newline. println() outputs with a newline.

quizzes.question:
What should go in the blank to display an integer using formatted output?
System.out.printf("The total is _____ yen", 1000);
quizzes.explanation: %d is the format specifier for embedding an integer. %s is for strings, and %f is for decimals.

lessons.title: What Are Variables?
lessons.summary: Understand the mechanism of naming and storing data

contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- What a [[variable]] is (a container for data)
- How to declare variables and assign values
- Variable naming rules (camelCase)

Variables are the most fundamental concept in programming.

contents.title: Declaring and Assigning Variables
contents.body:
Using a variable involves two steps:

1. Declaration: Create the variable (prepare the container)
2. Assignment: Store a value (put data into the container)

Performing both steps at the same time is called "initialization."

contents.title: Variables Are Like "Named Boxes"
contents.body:
Think of a variable as a "named box."

- Giving the box a name = declaring a variable
- Putting a value in the box = assigning a value
- Looking at what's inside the box = referencing a variable

You can also replace the value in the box later (reassignment).
contents.note: In Java, the convention is to use camelCase for variable names (e.g., myAge, totalCount).

contents.title: Using Variables
contents.body: Use the format: type variableName = value;
contents.note: The "//" in code is a comment (explanatory note). It does not affect program execution.

contents.title: Variable Naming Rules
contents.body:
- Must start with a letter, underscore (_), or dollar sign ($) — not a digit
- From the second character onward, letters, digits, underscores (_), and dollar signs ($) are allowed
- Names are case-sensitive (age and Age are different)
- Reserved words (class, int, for, etc.) cannot be used
- By convention, use camelCase (myAge, userName)
contents.note: Japanese variable names are technically possible, but English is standard in practice.

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- Create a variable with "type variableName = value;"
- Declaration (create the container) and assignment (store the value) are separate steps
- Doing both at once is called initialization
- camelCase is the convention for variable names

quizzes.question: The following code has an error. What is the cause?
quizzes.explanation: Variable names cannot start with a digit. They must begin with a letter, underscore (_), or dollar sign ($). number1 would be a valid variable name.

choices.text: int cannot be used as a type name
The variable name starts with a digit

quizzes.question: What is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: A variable's value can be overwritten (reassigned). Since x is first set to 10, then reassigned to 20, the output is 20.

quizzes.question: Which of the following is a valid variable name?
quizzes.explanation: myAge is a valid variable name in camelCase. my-age is invalid because hyphens are not allowed, class is a reserved word, and 3rdValue is invalid because it starts with a digit.

quizzes.question: Arrange the lines in the correct order to create a Java program that outputs "Hello World".
quizzes.explanation: A Java program is structured in this order: class declaration → main method → statements → closing braces.

lessons.title: Understanding Data Types
lessons.summary: How to use int, double, String, boolean, and char

contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Java's **primitive data types** (8 types)
- How to use integer, floating-point, character, and boolean types
- The size and value range of each type

Understanding [[data type]]s will help you choose the right [[variable]]s.

contents.title: Primitive Data Types
contents.body:
Java has 8 **primitive data types**, but let's start by learning these 4:

| Type | Description |
|---|---|
| int | Integer (approx. -2.1 billion to 2.1 billion) |
| double | Decimal (floating-point number) |
| boolean | Boolean value (true or false) |
| char | Single character (enclosed in single quotes) |
contents.note: There are also long (large integers), float (lower-precision decimals), byte, and short (smaller integers), but beginners can start with just int and double.

contents.title: Using Data Types
contents.body: Let's look at declaration examples for each type.
contents.code:
// Variable declaration and initialization
int age = 25;              // Integer
double price = 198.5;      // Decimal
boolean isStudent = true;  // Boolean value
char initial = 'A';        // 1Character
String name = "Taro";      // String(ReferenceType)
contents.note: String is not a **primitive data type** but a "**reference type**." However, it is designed to be especially easy to use. The uppercase S at the beginning is a telltale sign.

contents.title: Difference Between Primitive and Reference Types
contents.body:
Primitive types (int, double, etc.) hold the value itself. **Reference types** ([[String]], [[array]]s, etc.) hold the "location" of the data.

This distinction becomes important in later chapters, but for now, just remember that "String is special because it starts with an uppercase letter."

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- int (integer), double (decimal), boolean (true/false), char (single character) are the basics
- String (text) is a **reference type** but is used frequently
- char uses single quotes, String uses double quotes
- Assigning a value to a variable of a different type causes an error

quizzes.question: Which type is appropriate for handling decimal numbers?
quizzes.explanation: double is the type for handling decimal (floating-point) numbers. int is for integers only, boolean is for true/false values, and char is for single characters.

quizzes.question: The following code has an error. What is the cause?
quizzes.explanation: String values must be enclosed in double quotes ("). Single quotes (') are used for the char type (a single character).

choices.text: It is enclosed in single quotes
The variable name msg is invalid
The type name String is wrong

executionResult.errorMessage:
Main.java:2: error: unclosed character literal
        String msg = 'Hello';
                     ^
1 error

quizzes.question:
Complete the variable declaration for storing a boolean value.
_____ isReady = true;
quizzes.explanation: boolean is the type for handling true or false values. It is commonly used in conditional checks.

quizzes.question: Complete the code to assign a value to an int variable and print it.
quizzes.explanation: int is the integer type, and System.out.println() outputs with a newline. print() also works but outputs without a newline.

lessons.title: Calculations with Variables
lessons.summary: Understand arithmetic operators and operator precedence

contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- How [[type conversion]] ([[casting]]) works
- Automatic conversion (**implicit casting**) vs. **explicit casting**
- Being aware of data loss during type conversion

Understanding type conversion is important when mixing different [[data type]]s.

contents.title: Arithmetic Operators
contents.body: Here are the calculation symbols ([[operator]]s) available in Java.
contents.note: When dividing integers, the decimal part is truncated. 10 / 3 = 3, not 3.33...

contents.title: Watch the Direction of Type Conversion
contents.body:
Conversion from a smaller type to a larger type (widening conversion) happens automatically, but conversion from a larger type to a smaller type (narrowing conversion) requires an explicit cast.

| Direction | Example | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| Widening (automatic) | int → long, double | Value is preserved as-is |
| Narrowing (explicit) | double → int | Decimal part is truncated |

double d = 9.7; int i = (int) d; → i becomes 9 (not rounded).

contents.title: Compound Assignment Operators
contents.body: These are shorthand notations that perform a calculation and assignment at the same time.
contents.code:
// Variable declaration and initialization
int score = 100;
score += 10;  // score = score + 10;  → 110
score -= 20;  // score = score - 20;  → 90
score *= 2;   // score = score * 2;   → 180
score /= 3;   // score = score / 3;   → 60
score++;      // score = score + 1;   → 61
score--;      // score = score - 1;   → 60
contents.note: The // in code is a comment (explanatory note). It does not affect program behavior.

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- +  -  *  /  % are the five basic operators
- Integer division (/) truncates the decimal part
- % gives the remainder (useful for checking even numbers, etc.)
- +=, -=, ++, -- are shorthand notations

quizzes.question: What is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: When dividing int values, the decimal part is truncated. 7 ÷ 2 = 3.5, but since the type is int, the result is 3.

quizzes.question: What is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: x=10 → x+=5 makes it 15 → x*=2 makes it 30. Compound assignment operators are shorthand for "left = left operator right."

quizzes.question: What is the result of 10 % 3?
quizzes.explanation: % is the modulus operator, which gives the remainder of a division. 10 ÷ 3 = 3 remainder 1, so 10 % 3 = 1.

quizzes.question: Select all valid Java primitive types.
quizzes.explanation: int, boolean, and double are primitive types. String is a reference type, and Integer is a wrapper class for int (a reference type).

lessons.title: Working with Strings
lessons.summary: Learn how to concatenate, get the length of, and compare strings

contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Declaring and using constants (final)
- Why you should use constants (avoiding magic numbers)
- Naming conventions for constants (uppercase snake_case)

Protect values that should never change by making them "constants."

contents.title: String Concatenation
contents.body: You can join strings together using the + [[operator]].
contents.note: When you concatenate a string and a number with +, the number is automatically converted to a string.

contents.title: Constants Use Uppercase Snake Case
contents.body:
In Java, it is conventional to name constants (final variables) using uppercase snake_case.

- Correct examples: MAX_SIZE, TAX_RATE, DEFAULT_NAME
- Avoid: maxSize, taxrate, MAXSIZE

Adding final prevents reassignment. Replace "magic numbers" (mysterious numbers with unclear meaning) in your program with constants.
contents.note: Example of a magic number: if (price > 1000) → if (price > FREE_SHIPPING_THRESHOLD)

contents.title: Commonly Used String Methods
contents.body: String has many useful [[method]]s.
contents.note: The // in code is a comment (explanatory note). It does not affect program behavior.

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- Use the + operator to concatenate strings
- Use length() to get the number of characters
- Use equals() to compare strings (do not use ==)
- String is **immutable** — methods return a new string

contents.title: Searching and Extracting Strings
contents.body: Let's also learn methods for extracting parts of a string and searching for positions. These are frequently used in practice.
contents.note: The second argument of substring means "up to but not including that position." substring(0, 5) returns the 5 characters at positions 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4.

quizzes.question: What is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: length() returns the number of characters in a string. "Java" has 4 characters, so 4 is output.

quizzes.question: What is the correct way to compare strings?
quizzes.explanation: In Java, use the equals() method to compare the contents of strings. == compares references (memory locations), which can produce unexpected results.

quizzes.question: What is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: String + number is concatenated from left to right. "Value:" + 3 = "Value:3", then "Value:3" + 5 = "Value:35".

lessons.title: Type Conversion
lessons.summary: Learn the difference between automatic conversion and explicit casting

contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Basic operations on strings ([[String]])
- String concatenation, length retrieval, and comparison
- The difference between the equals method and the == [[operator]]

Strings are the most frequently used data in Java. Make sure you master the basic operations.

contents.title: Automatic Type Conversion (Implicit Conversion)
contents.body: Conversion from a smaller type to a larger type happens automatically.
contents.note: The "//" in code is a comment (explanatory note). It does not affect program execution.

contents.title: Casting (Explicit Conversion)
contents.body: Converting from a larger type to a smaller type requires you to explicitly write a [[cast]].
contents.note: Information can be lost during casting. For example, the decimal part is discarded when converting from double to int.

contents.title: Be Careful with == vs. equals()
contents.body:
This is the most common source of bugs in Java string comparison.

| Operator/Method | What It Compares | Usable for Strings? |
|---|---|---|
| == | Reference (memory location) | × May not compare correctly |
| equals() | Value (string content) | ○ Always compares correctly |

Always use equals() to compare strings.
contents.note: Primitive types like int and double can be compared with ==. equals() is used for comparing objects.

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
| Item | Description |
|---|---|
| Small → Large (widening) | Automatic conversion (e.g., int → double) |
| Large → Small (narrowing) | Cast required (e.g., (int)d format) |
| String → Number | Use Integer.parseInt(), etc. |
| Number → String | Use String.valueOf() |
- Information may be lost during casting

quizzes.question: What is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: When casting from double to int, the decimal part is truncated. 9.8 becomes 9 (it is not rounded).

quizzes.question: The following code has an error. What is the cause?
quizzes.explanation: Conversion from double to int involves potential data loss, so it is not done automatically. An explicit cast like (int) d is required.

choices.text: The variable name i is invalid

executionResult.errorMessage:
Main.java:3: error: incompatible types: possible lossy conversion from double to int
        int i = d;
                ^
1 error

quizzes.question:
Complete the code to convert the string "42" to an int value.
int num = Integer._____("42");
quizzes.explanation: Integer.parseInt() is the method that converts a string to an int. If you pass a string that cannot be converted, a NumberFormatException is thrown.
